notable_snippets: validateDestination(url): parses + normalizes URL, rejects userinfo, enforces https, resolves host -> IPs, denies private/link-local/metadata ranges, returns normalized {host,port,scheme} for client creation.
EgressPolicy.Allow(host, port, adapter): called once per client creation and on resolver refresh; deny triggers adapter circuit-breaker style failure with reason surfaced in status.
conversation: Marcus Lin (Security): "We should explicitly deny 169.254.169.254 (IMDS) even if we already block link-local. Also make sure we don't log full URLs (paths can contain sensitive identifiers)."
Jordan Lee (Author): "Added explicit metadata IP deny entries + tightened logging to only emit destination_id + reason. Also masked hostnames in debug logs unless trace is enabled."
Kevin Osei (Security): "Where is the validation enforced? Prefer fail-closed when strict validation is enabled, but don't break existing beta tenants silently."

Jordan Lee (Author): "When EXPORTER_STRICT_DEST_VALIDATION=1 we fail-closed and mark destination ERROR with reason. Default remains permissive until we flip per-tenant. Control plane will get matching validation in a separate PR; this is defense-in-depth."
Sean Gallagher (SRE): "DNS resolution caching: please bound refresh frequency and add a metric for denied events so we can alert if we accidentally block legit endpoints."
Jordan Lee (Author): "Refresh is min TTL=60s, max TTL=15m with jitter; added audit_exporter_egress_denied_total and destination_validation_failed_total."
Hanae Suzuki (Secrets/KMS): "Confirm we never include HEC tokens / Datadog API keys in error messages."
Jordan Lee (Author): "Confirmed; adapters only log destination_id and validation reason strings. No headers/tokens included."
Logan Wright (Observability): "Metric label cardinality: keep reasons bounded."
Jordan Lee (Author): "Reasons are enum-like (invalid_url, non_https, dns_failed, ip_private, ip_linklocal, ip_metadata, egress_policy_denied)."
Marcus Lin (Security) - Approval: "Looks good for beta gating. Please ensure we document the default-off behavior and the plan to flip per tenant."
Sean Gallagher (SRE) - Approval: "OK with gating; metrics look usable."
Kevin Osei (Security) - Approval: "Ship it with strict flag off by default."
source.release_notes: Security hardening for audit exporter destinations: adds destination URL validation and pluggable egress allowlist enforcement (beta-gated via EXPORTER_STRICT_DEST_VALIDATION).

source.title: Support attaching retention config export to audit log evidence package
source.author: Kevin Osei
source.release_notes: Audit log exports can optionally include a retention configuration attachment (JSON) in the same evidence package to reduce back-and-forth in security reviews.
source.body:
Context
Customers (and GRC internally) frequently request both (a) audit logs for a specified window and (b) an export of the tenant’s retention configuration (incl. defaults + overrides + change history pointer) as a single evidence package. This PR adds a first-class way to bundle the retention config export alongside the audit log export output.

Scope
- Add optional attachment support to evidence packages produced by audit-log-exporter.
- Implement a RetentionConfigAttachmentProvider which fetches retention config export from the control plane export endpoint and embeds it in the package.
- Update report template runner to allow "attachments" definitions (initially: retention_config).
- Add guardrails: size limits, explicit allowlist, and redaction check for known sensitive fields.
- Update CLI + HTTP API to accept the attachment flag(s).

Design notes
- Attachments are stored within the package as additional files under evidence/attachments/.
- Retention config is included as JSON (versioned) + a small README describing what it is and how it’s generated.
- The exporter is intentionally not doing policy evaluation; it only fetches the export artifact produced by the retention policy service (control plane).
- All attachment requests are recorded in the exporter’s own audit event stream (EXPORT_ATTACHMENT_ADDED) for traceability.

Usage
HTTP API:
- POST /v1/exports
  - body: { "template_id": "customer_evidence_v1", "time_window": {"start": "...", "end": "..."}, "attachments": ["retention_config"] }

CLI:
- audit-log-exporter export --template customer_evidence_v1 --start 2025-02-01T00:00:00Z --end 2025-02-15T00:00:00Z --attach retention_config

Validation / guardrails
- attachments must be from a static allowlist.
- retention config attachment requires control-plane auth scope: retention:export (in addition to audit:export).
- max attachment size: 2 MiB (hard fail; error code EXPORT_ATTACHMENT_TOO_LARGE).
- package builder refuses to overwrite an existing file path (defense-in-depth).

Testing
- Unit: attachment path sanitation, allowlist validation, size checks.
- Integration: mocked control plane export response (v1 + v1.1) and package contents verification.
- E2E (staging): export generated for Northbridge trial tenant; verified package contains audit_logs.csv + attachments/retention_config.json + attachments/README-retention-config.txt.

Rollout
- Feature-gated behind EXPORT_ATTACHMENTS_ENABLED (default off).
- Enabled in staging immediately; production enablement after Security sign-off on package contents.

References
- Linear: ENG-18434 (audit logs retention enforcement), ENG-18441 (compliance report pack generator)
- Support signal: SUP-3842 (enterprise customer requests retention controls + report pack)
- Related exporter work: PR-612 (time-windowed exports and filters)

Open questions (tracked in ENG-18441)
- Whether to include retention config change history in the attachment vs. only a pointer/link to download separately.
- Whether to also attach data residency/region summary as a separate attachment type (likely next).
commits: feat(package): introduce attachment interface and evidence package bundling
feat(retention): add provider to fetch retention config export from control plane
feat(api): accept attachments[] in export create request + validate allowlist
feat(cli): add --attach flag and propagate to API payload
security: enforce size limits + path traversal protection for attachment filenames
test: unit + integration coverage for attachment validation and packaging
conversation: Marcus Lin (review): Can we confirm the retention config export does not include any prompt/completion payloads or request bodies? Also want an explicit size cap and a path traversal check for attachment filenames.

Kevin Osei (author): Confirmed: attachment uses control plane export endpoint that only returns retention policy config + metadata (no content logs). Added 2 MiB cap (hard fail) and normalized all attachment paths to a fixed directory; also added a test for '../' traversal.

Olga Petrov (review): For customer evidence, we need the attachment to be self-describing. Can you include a short README in the package describing what the retention config is, timestamp, and how to interpret the fields (tier defaults vs overrides)?

Kevin Osei (author): Added attachments/README-retention-config.txt including export timestamp, tenant id, schema version, and notes on defaults vs overrides. Also included the control plane endpoint name and required scope for traceability.

Ethan Park (review): This pulls from control plane; how are we handling auth and failures? We should fail the export if attachment is requested but cannot be fetched, and the error should be explicit. Also: are we caching retention config export? Please don’t add caching here.

Kevin Osei (author): No caching in exporter. If attachment fetch fails, export is marked failed with EXPORT_ATTACHMENT_FETCH_FAILED including upstream status. If attachments not requested, baseline behavior unchanged.
Marcus Lin (review): Add an exporter-side audit event when an attachment is added. This matters for internal traceability when customers ask what was included in evidence packages.
Kevin Osei (author): Added EXPORT_ATTACHMENT_ADDED event with attachment_type, export_id, actor, and created_at. Wired to existing event sink.
Olga Petrov (approval): Looks good. This matches what we need for the compliance reporting pack MVP (single package, fewer manual steps). Approving pending CI green.
Marcus Lin (approval): Approved after the size/path safety checks landed. Please keep the feature flag default off until we sign off on production contents.
Kevin Osei (author): Acknowledged; default remains off, staging enabled only.

source.title: Add time-windowed exports + actor/event-type filters (report templates)
source.author: Kevin Osei
source.release_notes: Audit log exporter now supports time-windowed exports and optional actor + event-type filtering. Adds report-template presets used for customer evidence packages; default behavior unchanged when filters are not provided.
source.body:
## Summary
This PR enhances `audit-log-exporter` to support compliance/evidence workflows by adding (1) explicit time-windowed exports and (2) optional filters for actor + event types. It also introduces a small report-template layer so the compliance reporting pack can request a known preset ("last_90_days_admin_activity", "rbac_changes", etc.) without re-encoding filter logic in multiple places.

Motivation: enterprise security reviews are consistently asking for "audit logs for a defined period" and "only admin/security-relevant events". We also had a support report (SUP-3917) where customers believed exports were missing event types; part of that was unclear defaults and lack of an explicit event-type filter list in the export request.

## API changes
### New request fields (non-breaking)
- `start_time` (RFC3339, inclusive)
- `end_time` (RFC3339, exclusive)
- `actor_ids` (optional array of strings)
- `actor_types` (optional array: `user|service_account|api_key`)
- `event_types` (optional array of strings; validated against known types)
- `template_id` (optional string; mutually exclusive with providing filters directly unless `allow_override=true`)

Behavior:
- If `start_time`/`end_time` are omitted, behavior remains "export all available within retention" (existing behavior).
- If provided, exporter enforces the window at query time and re-checks in stream/iteration to avoid boundary drift.
- `event_types` is an allowlist filter; unknown types return 400 with a list of valid types.
- `actor_ids` and `actor_types` are ANDed with the time-window and event filter.

### Report templates
Adds `templates/*.yaml` defining a stable set of presets that map to filters + default time windows. Example preset:
- `rbac_changes_last_180d` -> `event_types=[rbac.role.created, rbac.role.updated, rbac.binding.created, rbac.binding.deleted]`, window=180d

Template contract:
- Templates are versioned by exporter release; template IDs are stable.
- Export response includes `applied_filters` so customers/auditors can see exactly what was used.

## Implementation notes
- Query layer updated to accept a `FilterSpec` that compiles to SQL WHERE clauses + parameter binding.
- Streaming export code now emits a header block with: `export_id`, `generated_at`, `window`, `filters`, `template_id` (if any).
- Added a canonical "known event types" registry and tests ensuring registry coverage matches the producer schema we expect.
- Pagination: time-windowed exports use `(timestamp, id)` cursor to avoid duplicates when multiple events share the same timestamp.

## Security/Compliance considerations
- Filters are validated server-side (no raw SQL fragments; strict param binding).
- `actor_ids` is limited to 200 entries to cap query size.
- Maximum export window defaults to 365 days unless `--allow-large-windows` is enabled (intended for internal GRC workflows only).
- Added explicit audit event for `audit_log.export.generated` that records: caller identity, window, filters, and bytes written.

## Testing
- Unit tests: filter compilation, RFC3339 parsing, template resolution, unknown event-type validation.
- Integration tests: seeded audit events across boundary timestamps; verifies inclusive/exclusive semantics.
- Load sanity: ran export for 90d in staging with 1.2M rows; query time stable and memory constant in streaming mode.

## Rollout
- Default behavior unchanged. Consumers can adopt templates incrementally.
- Follow-up: wire `template_id` usage into compliance report pack generator (ENG-18441) once this merges.

## Example request
`POST /v1/exports`
{
  "template_id": "rbac_changes_last_180d",
  "start_time": "2024-08-01T00:00:00Z",
  "end_time": "2025-02-01T00:00:00Z"
}

## Checklist
- [x] Backwards compatible
- [x] Tests added
- [x] Docs updated (README + examples)
- [x] Security review notes captured (filters + limits)
- [x] Added exporter-side audit event for export generation
commits: feat(filters): add start/end time window parsing + validation
feat(filters): actor_id / actor_type / event_type allowlist filtering
feat(templates): add template loader + built-in presets
fix(pagination): use (timestamp,id) cursor for deterministic iteration
test: integration coverage for boundary semantics and template overrides
docs: update README with new fields + examples
review_comments: Marcus Lin: Please confirm window semantics (inclusive/exclusive) are documented and tested, and ensure we’re not vulnerable to massive `actor_ids` payloads. Also: do we need an exporter audit event for generating an export?

Kevin Osei (author): Documented in README + added integration tests for inclusive start/exclusive end. Added max 200 actor IDs and a request size cap. Implemented `audit_log.export.generated` event emitted on successful export start (includes window/filters/template).
Olga Petrov: Template IDs need to be stable and customer-safe. Can we include the applied filters in the export output so auditors don’t have to trust our template mapping?

Kevin Osei (author): Added `applied_filters` header block (JSON) at the start of the exported artifact and also in the API response metadata. Template IDs are now validated against a registry and treated as public identifiers.

Sean Gallagher: Cursor should be deterministic. Using timestamp-only can duplicate/skip on high volume; recommend (timestamp,id) ordering and ensure we have an index. Also add guardrails for >365d windows.
Kevin Osei (author): Updated pagination to order by `(timestamp, id)` and added/verified index in migrations. Added 365d default max window with an explicit server flag for internal workflows.
source.merge_summary: Merged via squash after 3 approvals. Follow-ups tracked in ENG-18441 for compliance pack wiring and in SUP-3917 notes to point customers at event-type filtering + applied_filters metadata.

- Unit tests: filter compilation, RFC3339 parsing, template resolution, unknown event-type validation.
- Integration tests: seeded audit events across boundary timestamps; verifies inclusive/exclusive semantics.

- Default behavior unchanged. Consumers can adopt templates incrementally.
- Follow-up: wire `template_id` usage into compliance report pack generator (ENG-18441) once this merges.

## Example request
`POST /v1/exports`
{
  "template_id": "rbac_changes_last_180d",
  "start_time": "2024-08-01T00:00:00Z",
  "end_time": "2025-02-01T00:00:00Z"
}

- [x] Backwards compatible
- [x] Tests added
- [x] Docs updated (README + examples)
- [x] Security review notes captured (filters + limits)
- [x] Added exporter-side audit event for export generation

## Summary
This PR enhances `audit-log-exporter` to support compliance/evidence workflows by adding (1) explicit time-windowed exports and (2) optional filters for actor + event types. It also introduces a small report-template layer so the compliance reporting pack can request a known preset ("last_90_days_admin_activity", "rbac_changes", etc.) without re-encoding filter logic in multiple places.

Motivation: enterprise security reviews are consistently asking for "audit logs for a defined period" and "only admin/security-relevant events". We also had a support report (SUP-3917) where customers believed exports were missing event types; part of that was unclear defaults and lack of an explicit event-type filter list in the export request.

## API changes
### New request fields (non-breaking)
- `start_time` (RFC3339, inclusive)
- `end_time` (RFC3339, exclusive)
- `actor_ids` (optional array of strings)
- `actor_types` (optional array: `user|service_account|api_key`)
- `event_types` (optional array of strings; validated against known types)
- `template_id` (optional string; mutually exclusive with providing filters directly unless `allow_override=true`)

Behavior:
- If `start_time`/`end_time` are omitted, behavior remains "export all available within retention" (existing behavior).
- If provided, exporter enforces the window at query time and re-checks in stream/iteration to avoid boundary drift.

source.title: Backpressure controls + max lag safety (auto-pause) for downstream outages
source.author: Aisha Bello
source.description:
Context: We had a beta incident where Splunk HEC throttling (429 + intermittent 503) caused exporter lag to spike and sustained retries increased queue pressure and CPU, impacting other tenants sharing the same exporter shard (see #incidents thread 1739559010 and SUP-18531 follow-up). This PR introduces explicit backpressure controls and a max-lag safety mechanism to keep the system stable during downstream outages while preserving at-least-once semantics.

What’s in this PR: (1) Max lag threshold per destination with auto-pause: if export_lag_seconds for a (tenant, destination) exceeds configurable max_lag_seconds for a sustained window, we stop pulling new batches for that destination and mark it PAUSED (reason=MAX_LAG) while continuing to process other destinations/tenants. (2) Backpressure based on in-flight bytes and retry backlog: introduce per-destination in_flight_bytes limits and a bounded retry queue; when exceeded we apply pull-side backoff rather than pushing more work into retries. (3) Circuit breaker integration for adapters: adapter-specific throttling signals (e.g., Splunk 429, Datadog 429/5xx) now feed a shared circuit breaker that increases backoff, and can trip into PAUSED when combined with lag growth. (4) Operator controls: new CLI commands to unpause a destination and to override max lag for controlled recovery. (5) Observability: new Prometheus metrics and structured log fields for pause/unpause transitions, reasons, and “time paused”; status endpoint now includes pause state and last_pause_reason.

Delivery semantics: No events are dropped. Auto-pause stops the exporter from advancing its checkpoint for the paused destination, so events remain available for replay/backfill once the downstream recovers and the destination is unpaused. This is consistent with our at-least-once contract; downstream dedup remains based on event_id.

Configuration: Adds exporter config keys (with safe defaults): max_lag_seconds (default 21600 / 6h), max_lag_grace_period_seconds (default 300), max_in_flight_bytes (default 64MiB), max_retry_queue_batches (default 500), pause_cooldown_seconds (default 900). All are feature-flagged behind BACKPRESSURE_V1 to limit beta blast radius.

Testing: unit tests for PauseController transitions; integration test with Splunk stub that returns sustained 429 and verifies: lag rises -> pause triggers -> pulling stops -> unpause resumes and drains; chaos-ish test ensures healthy destination continues despite one paused. Load test notes included in commit message, based on SRE backpressure experiments doc.

Rollout plan: Enable BACKPRESSURE_V1 for beta tenants only after deploying exporter + dashboards. Default max lag is conservative; SRE can tune per-tenant via config overrides if needed.

Checklist: [x] Metrics + dashboards hooks (exporter_paused, pause_reason, pause_duration_seconds) [x] Runbook updates TODO in separate PR (Sean requested) [x] Security review: no new PII; logs redact destination secrets [x] Feature flag gated (BACKPRESSURE_V1) [x] Integration tests cover 429/5xx.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): (a) surface pause/unpause controls in Console (control plane) (b) alerting thresholds tuning once we have 1-2 weeks of beta data (c) doc updates for customer-facing explanation of auto-pause + recovery expectations.
commits: Add PauseController and destination state machine (RUNNING/PAUSED) with reasons
Implement max lag monitor and grace period windowing
Bound retry queue + in-flight bytes limiter; apply pull-side backoff
Wire adapter throttle signals into shared circuit breaker
Expose pause state in /healthz and /status endpoints
Add Prometheus metrics + structured logs for pause transitions
Add integration tests: sustained 429 triggers pause, unpause resumes drain
Config plumbing + BACKPRESSURE_V1 feature flag + docs in README (internal)
ci_checks: integration-tests (splunk stub): pass (rerun once due to flaky timing)
conversation: Sean Gallagher (review): The pause semantics look right, but can you confirm we don’t advance checkpoints while paused? Also please make sure we page on “paused for too long” only after GA; for beta we should ticket unless it’s multi-tenant impact.

Aisha Bello (author): Confirmed: when state=PAUSED we stop dequeuing batches and we do not commit destination checkpoints. Added an explicit guard + test asserting checkpoint version doesn’t change while paused. Alerting not part of this PR; I’ll coordinate with Logan/Allison on rules in observability-pack.
Logan Wright (review): Metrics naming: can we align with the exporter dashboard conventions from PR-1098? Specifically avoid high-cardinality labels. tenant_id as a label is risky.

Aisha Bello (author): Updated: tenant_id removed from metrics labels; we emit tenant_id only in structured logs and tracing attributes. Metrics are now exporter_destination_paused{destination_type,reason} and exporter_export_lag_seconds{destination_type} with per-tenant breakdown available via logs/traces.
Marcus Lin (security review): Please ensure pause/unpause logs don’t include destination URLs or tokens; also verify that “reason” values are from a fixed allowlist (no raw error messages).

Aisha Bello (author): Done. reason is an enum (MAX_LAG, CIRCUIT_BREAKER, IN_FLIGHT_LIMIT, RETRY_BACKLOG, OPERATOR). Logs include destination_id (UUID) only; URL/token are never logged. Added a redaction test for config serialization.
Rafael Mendes (review): I’d like an operator escape hatch. If we pause on max lag, how do we recover without redeploy? CLI command is fine for now.

Aisha Bello (author): Added `exporterctl destinations unpause --tenant <t> --destination <id>` and `exporterctl destinations pause --reason OPERATOR` for manual mitigation. Commands require operator auth in our deployment (same as existing replay tooling).
Sean Gallagher (follow-up): The integration test sleeps a lot; can we make it less timing-dependent?

Aisha Bello (author): Reduced wall clock by using a fake clock in lag monitor unit tests. Integration test now uses tighter backoff intervals under test config and asserts on state transitions with retries rather than fixed sleeps.
release_notes: Exporter now auto-pauses destinations when export lag exceeds a configurable threshold to protect stability during downstream outages.
New backpressure controls bound in-flight bytes and retry backlog; improved behavior under sustained 429/5xx from SIEM sinks.
New metrics and status fields expose pause state and reasons for operational visibility.

source.title: Bootstrap audit exporter service skeleton + CI + local dev harness
source.author: Diego Santos
source.release_notes: Internal-only: introduces new audit-log-exporter service scaffold and CI; no production impact yet (feature-flagged downstream).
source.body:
## Summary
Bootstraps the new `audit-log-exporter` service repository with a minimal runnable skeleton, CI workflows, a base configuration model, and a local dev harness to support early iteration.

This is the first landing step for ENG-5139. No adapters, no delivery semantics, and no production wiring in this PR; those will follow in subsequent PRs.

## Motivation / Context
We need a dedicated service to read audit events (initially via stubbed source in local dev), apply tenant scoping, and deliver to downstream destinations with retries/checkpointing. Before we can iterate on architecture and adapters, we need:
- Standard project layout + build tooling
- Baseline CI (lint/test/build, container build)
- A config model (typed) that can evolve with control-plane config sync
- A local harness so devs can run the service end-to-end without needing the full control plane

## What’s included
- Service scaffold (Go)
  - `cmd/audit-exporter` main entrypoint
  - `internal/server` HTTP server with `/healthz` + `/readyz`
  - `internal/config` base config model + validation
  - `internal/logging` structured logging setup (json)
- CI / tooling
  - GitHub Actions for: `go test`, `go vet`, `golangci-lint`, `gofmt` check, and Docker build
  - Dependabot for Go modules + GitHub Actions
  - Makefile targets: `make test`, `make lint`, `make run`, `make docker-build`
- Local dev harness
  - `docker-compose.yml` to run exporter + a lightweight stub source (placeholder) + optional Prometheus
  - `./scripts/dev/generate_synthetic_events.sh` to emit sample audit events to stdout/file (temporary)
- Base config model (initial)
  - `listen_addr`, `log_level`
  - `tenant_id` (dev-only for now; will be replaced by multi-tenant config sync)
  - placeholder `export` section for future destinations (intentionally not wired)

## Not included (intentionally)
- No real event ingestion from Redwood services yet
- No destination adapters (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
- No checkpointing / delivery guarantees
- No filtering engine
- No secret management integration

## Testing
- Unit tests for config parsing + validation
- CI builds container image to ensure Dockerfile stays green
- Manual:
  - `make run` (starts HTTP server)
  - `docker compose up --build` (runs exporter + harness components)

## Follow-ups
- ENG-5132: Control plane export config API
- ENG-5144/5151/5156: Destination adapters
- ENG-5168: Metrics + tracing
- ENG-5189: Threat model items (egress allowlist hooks, secrets handling)

## Notes for reviewers
- Config model is deliberately minimal and will churn; goal is to establish patterns (validation, defaults, env overrides).
- Health endpoints are required for upcoming k8s deployment.

Closes: ENG-5139 (bootstrap portion)
commits: chore(repo): initial module setup + README + project layout
build(ci): add GitHub Actions for lint/test/build + docker build
feat(server): add healthz/readyz endpoints and graceful shutdown
feat(config): base config structs, env/file parsing, validation + tests
chore(dev): add docker-compose harness and synthetic event generator script
conversation: Ethan Park (review): Looks good for the bootstrap. Two asks before merge: (1) please add `--config` flag support (file path) so we can align with how the control plane services do it, and (2) ensure readiness fails if config validation fails (don’t just log + continue).

Diego Santos (author): Ack. I added a `--config` flag (default `./config/local.yaml` if present, else env-only) and changed startup to fail fast on invalid config. Readiness now reports not-ready until the server is fully started (but startup exits on invalid config anyway).
Logan Wright (review): For observability: can we at least standardize request logging fields and reserve a metrics port? Even if metrics come later, I’d like the server to have a stable pattern.

Diego Santos (author): Added consistent log fields (`service`, `version`, `request_id`) and split `listen_addr` vs `admin_addr` (admin serves health endpoints for now; metrics can land there in ENG-5168).
Marcus Lin (review): Please confirm we’re not logging any raw event payloads in this PR. Even in dev harness, we should avoid setting a precedent.

Diego Santos (author): Confirmed. No event payload logging in service code; the synthetic generator writes to a local file for dev only. Updated README to call out that event contents must not be logged by default and that PII redaction rules apply once we ingest real events.
Sean Gallagher (review): Can you add graceful shutdown handling (SIGTERM) and a configurable shutdown timeout? We’ll need that for deployments + rolling restarts.
Diego Santos (author): Added context-aware shutdown with a `shutdown_timeout` config (default 15s).
Ethan Park (approval): Approved. This is a good base to build on. Please keep next PRs small (config sync, adapters, etc.) so we can review quickly.
Logan Wright (approval): Approved after admin_addr change. Please tag metrics naming conventions in the ENG-5168 PR.
Marcus Lin (approval): Approved with the no-payload-logging note in README.

source.author: Benji Okafor
source.description:
Implements config sync from the control plane for the audit exporter service. This adds (1) tenant scoping so a given exporter instance only processes configs it is authorized to see, (2) a resilient polling loop with jitter/backoff and a long-poll “watch” mode when supported by the control plane, and (3) an in-memory snapshot cache with safe defaults so we do not accidentally start exporting for a tenant unless the config is explicitly enabled.

Motivation
- ENG-5139 created the exporter scaffold; we need an end-to-end way to pull export destinations/filters from the control plane without requiring restarts.
- ENG-5132 is landing CRUD + validation in the control plane; this PR wires up the consumer side.

Key behaviors
- Startup: exporter boots in “no tenants enabled” state until it successfully fetches a config snapshot.
- Polling: periodic snapshot fetch (default 10s with +/-20% jitter) with exponential backoff on failures.
- Streaming/watch: if the control plane advertises watch support, we keep an open long-poll to get revision notifications and then refresh the snapshot (keeps overhead low while staying responsive).
- Tenant scoping: requests use an exporter service identity; control plane response is already filtered to authorized tenants. Exporter additionally enforces that it will only instantiate pipelines for tenants present in the snapshot and will tear down pipelines if a tenant disappears from the snapshot.
- Safe defaults: configs without destinations, without enabled=true, or with invalid/missing credential refs are treated as disabled and surfaced in health/status metrics (no export attempts).
- Caching: configs are stored as an atomic snapshot; adapters read from a read-only view to avoid lock contention.

Notes
- This PR intentionally does not fetch any secret material. Destination configs refer to secret references (e.g., vault/KMS-managed identifiers) that adapters will resolve through the existing secret provider interface.
- Filtering semantics are not implemented here (ENG-5160). We pass through filter blobs to keep the boundary stable.

Follow-ups
- Wire config revision + export status endpoint (lag/last error) once the exporter metrics PR lands.
- Add more “thundering herd” mitigation once we have multi-replica deployments (per-instance staggered start already included).
commits: configsync: add ControlPlaneClient with snapshot + watch endpoints
configsync: implement poll loop with jitter + exponential backoff
tenancy: enforce tenant scoping + pipeline teardown on config removal
cache: add atomic snapshot store + config diffing for minimal churn
health: surface last successful sync + last error + revision in /healthz
tests: add fake control plane server + sync loop unit tests
docs: local dev notes for running exporter against control-plane stub
changed_files: internal/controlplane/client.go (HTTP client, auth headers, retry policy)
internal/adapters/registry.go (accept config snapshot view, safe defaults)
docs/dev/config-sync.md (how to run with stubbed control plane)

2025-02-11 13:02 Ethan Park: Couple questions: (1) what’s the contract if the control plane is unavailable at boot? (2) do we ever accidentally export with stale config?

source.title: Add Datadog Logs destination adapter + audit tag/facet mapping
source.author: Caleb Johnson
source.description: Summary: This PR adds a Datadog Logs destination adapter to the audit-log-exporter and establishes a stable mapping from audit event schema v2 -> Datadog tags/facets. It also introduces rate-limit aware batching behavior (primarily for 429/503) so we degrade gracefully under Datadog intake constraints while preserving at-least-once semantics. Context: As part of the SIEM-ready audit export beta, we support Splunk HEC, S3, and Datadog Logs. Datadog differs from Splunk in that (a) intake is strongly rate-limited, and (b) the best user experience is via consistent tagging/faceting for filtering/dashboards in Datadog. This PR aligns with ENG-5156 and the delivery semantics ADR (at-least-once, downstream dedup via event_id). Design notes (high-level): Adapter: Adds `datadog_logs` destination type with `site`, `intake_path`, and `api_key_secret_ref`. We send batches to `https://http-intake.logs.{site}/api/v2/logs` (default) with JSON payloads. Each audit event becomes one log item with a message, attributes, and tags. Tag/facet mapping: We map audit event fields into a consistent set of Datadog tags for filtering. Primary tags: `redwood.tenant_id`, `redwood.workspace_id`, `redwood.category`, `redwood.action`, `redwood.resource_type`, `redwood.actor_type`, and optionally `redwood.actor_id` (only when allowed by audit policy). We also set `service=redwood-audit-export`, `ddsource=redwood`, and `env=<region|deployment>` (exporter runtime env). We keep the raw payload minimal: we do not include request bodies, prompt text, or free-form metadata; event context is limited to schema v2 allowed fields and pre-existing redactions. Rate-limit aware batching: The adapter maintains a per-destination adaptive batch size. On 429, we back off with jitter and reduce batch size (floor 1). On success, we slowly increase toward the configured max (AIMD-like behavior) to recover throughput without oscillation. We also respect `Retry-After` when present and otherwise default to exponential backoff using the exporter retry policy. Checkpointing/dedup: We do not attempt exactly-once delivery. Each log item includes `event_id` as `redwood.event_id` (tag + attribute) so customers can dedup. We preserve ordering within a partition best-effort but do not guarantee strict order under retries. Security/PII: The adapter redacts sensitive fields in logs and avoids logging payload contents. Exporter logs include only: destination id, batch size, response status, dd request id (when provided), and retry wait. API key is loaded via secret ref; no key material in config logs. Tests: Adds unit tests for tag mapping (category->tag), and integration-ish tests via stubbed intake server covering 200, 429 with Retry-After, and 503 transient errors. Operators: Adds additional metrics for adapter status codes and rate-limit backoff behavior to help SRE tune defaults. Rollout: Feature-flagged behind destination validation at control plane; exporter supports the destination type when present in config sync. Follow-ups: (1) support additional Datadog intake paths for Gov/US3 sites (config already allows override); (2) add customer-facing docs and a Console “Test connection” for Datadog (tracked elsewhere).
commits: adapter/datadog: scaffold DatadogLogsAdapter + config parsing
adapter/datadog: implement v2 logs intake client + request/response handling
mapping: add audit schema v2 -> dd tags/facets mapping helpers
retry: add rate-limit aware batch sizing + Retry-After support
metrics: add datadog adapter counters + backoff gauges
tests: add mapping unit tests + stubbed intake tests
docs: update destination type registry + local dev examples
notable_diff_snippets: Rate-limit behavior: on HTTP 429 -> if Retry-After present, sleep that duration; else exponential backoff (base=1s, max=60s) with jitter; reduce batch_size = max(floor, ceil(batch_size/2)); on success -> increase batch_size by +10% up to configured max.
source.conversation: Logan Wright (review): Can we standardize the tag keys to avoid later migration? In other exporters we use `redwood.category` not `category`. Also please ensure `event_id` is present both in tags and as a structured attribute for facet creation. Caleb Johnson (author): Updated mapping to always prefix `redwood.` and added `redwood.event_id` as both tag + attribute. Also added a unit test asserting presence. Marcus Lin (review): Please confirm we’re not sending user agent / IP in a way that violates the audit PII policy. Datadog is not a “safe sink” by default for verbose fields. Also make sure exporter logs never include the payload. Caleb Johnson (author): Verified: payload includes only schema v2 allowed fields. IP/user_agent are only included when present in the canonical audit event and already subject to redaction rules upstream; this adapter does not add new fields. Exporter structured logs now explicitly omit payload and only log `dd_request_id`, status, and counts. Added a test that logger does not emit the serialized request body. Sean Gallagher (review): Rate-limit aware batching looks good but I’m concerned about thundering herd across tenants when DD is down. Do we have per-destination jitter? Also please add a metric we can alert on to detect sustained 429s. Caleb Johnson (author): Added jitter to all retry sleeps (full jitter capped by base delay). Added `audit_export_adapter_rate_limited_total{adapter="datadog"}` and a gauge `audit_export_adapter_backoff_seconds{adapter="datadog"}`. Colin O'Donnell (review): Customer expectation: they’ll want to filter by category/action quickly in Datadog. Can we include `action` as a top-level attribute too (not just tag), so facets are straightforward? Caleb Johnson (author): Added `redwood.action` attribute mirror alongside tag. Also added docs note on creating facets in Datadog for `@redwood.action` and `@redwood.category`.
source.release_notes: Enterprise: Audit log export now supports Datadog Logs as a destination (beta). Includes consistent tag/facet mapping for audit categories/actions and improved handling of Datadog intake rate limits (429/503) with adaptive batching + backoff.

source.title: Filtering engine v1: allowlist/denylist by category/action/resource
source.author: Jordan Lee
source.release_notes: Beta: adds exporter-side audit event filtering (allow/deny rules) with strict config validation and unit test coverage for edge cases.
source.description: Implements filtering engine v1 for SIEM-ready audit export (ENG-5160). This adds a small, explicit rule model (allowlist/denylist) that can be evaluated per-event in the exporter with deterministic semantics and safe defaults. Scope in this PR: (1) filter model + evaluation logic (categories/actions/resource predicates), (2) strict config validation + normalization (including “deny wins” and invalid rule rejection), and (3) comprehensive unit tests for tricky cases (empty filters, conflicting allow/deny, unknown categories, wildcard handling, resource/action normalization, timestamp boundary behavior). Motivation: we need customer-configurable filtering that is auditable, easy to reason about during incident response, and stable enough to map to UI + control-plane API without frequent behavior changes. Semantics (as implemented): The effective policy is evaluated in this order: validate -> normalize -> evaluate per event. If filters are absent/null, exporter passes all events (current beta default). If allowlist rules exist, an event must match at least one allow rule to be included. Deny rules always override allow rules (i.e., if an event matches any deny rule, it is excluded even if it also matches allow). Matching: category/action are exact match against canonical strings; resource matching supports resource.type exact match, and resource.id supports either exact match or a simple prefix pattern of the form "prefix:*" (to support common shapes like "workspace:*" without introducing a full glob/regex surface). If a rule specifies multiple fields, the rule matches only if all specified fields match (AND). If a filter list contains unknown categories/actions (not in the exporter’s current taxonomy allowlist), config is rejected and exporter will not start that tenant’s pipeline (surfaced as config error + status). This is intentional for beta to avoid silently dropping/including unexpected events; we can revisit “unknown = no match” for GA if we need forward-compat behavior. Notes: This PR is aligned with ADR-audit-export-filtering-model (allow/deny + deterministic evaluation) and keeps the API surface minimal. The control plane is expected to perform the same validation; exporter validation is defense-in-depth in case of stale/buggy configs. Follow-ups: (a) wire up exporter status endpoint to include “filter_validation_error” details (truncated/safe) for Console, (b) add microbench for high-volume match evaluation, (c) consider forward-compat for new categories (feature-flagged behavior) once schema v2 taxonomy is stable. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for matching + precedence - [x] Validation errors are structured and redact sensitive fields - [x] Metrics: counter for filtered_out_total by tenant + category (bounded cardinality) - [x] Docs snippet added to internal README for config examples - [ ] E2E coverage will land in ENG-5195 (separate PR)
commits: filter: introduce FilterSpec + normalized FilterPolicy
filter: implement matcher for category/action/resource predicates
validation: reject unknown categories/actions; enforce max rule counts
metrics: add audit_export_filter_filtered_out_total
tests: add table-driven tests for precedence + wildcard prefix
docs: add config examples + semantics notes
code_snippets: Pseudo-config example used in tests: allow: [{category: "auth", action: "token.create"}, {category: "admin", resource_type: "audit_export"}] deny: [{category: "admin", action: "rbac.policy.update"}, {resource_type: "workspace", resource_id: "workspace:prod-*"}] // deny wins; allow required if allowlist non-empty
review_conversation: Marcus Lin (Security): Can we confirm deny > allow is explicit and tested? Also, please ensure validation errors don’t echo full resource IDs (could be sensitive).

Jordan Lee (Author): Yes—added table-driven tests for overlap and a unit test asserting deny wins. Validation errors now include only rule index + field name + reason; resource_id values are not printed (only length + whether prefix-pattern was used).
Ethan Park (Platform): How strict should we be about unknown categories/actions? If schema adds a new category, we might block exports until the exporter is updated.

Jordan Lee (Author): Agreed risk. For beta I leaned strict to avoid “silent wrong” behavior. I added a TODO + comment in validation.go to revisit and potentially feature-flag forward-compat (unknown => no match) once taxonomy stabilizes. This also matches what Console will display (explicit config error).
Benji Okafor (Control Plane): Please document max rule counts and the prefix pattern format. UI needs to mirror this. Also confirm matcher is allocation-free in the hot path.

Jordan Lee (Author): Updated README with max rules (allow<=50, deny<=50 per config, total<=100) and prefix pattern is "<prefix>*" with a required trailing "*". Matcher uses pre-normalized structs + string comparisons; no regex and no per-event allocations in the core match loop (verified with go test -run TestMatcher_NoAllocs).
Kevin Osei (Security/Compliance): One more ask: include metric for filtered events, but keep labels bounded. Don’t label on action/resource_id.
Jordan Lee (Author): Done—metric is filtered_out_total labeled by tenant_id (internal), destination_id (small bounded set per tenant), and category only. No action/resource labels.
Marcus Lin (Security): Approved once the error messages are confirmed redacted.
Jordan Lee (Author): Pushed follow-up commit; errors now use "rule[3].resource_id: invalid prefix pattern" without values. Added tests that assert error string does not contain provided resource IDs.
Ethan Park (Platform): LGTM.
Marcus Lin (Security): LGTM.
source.post_merge_notes: Reminder: keep exporter + control-plane validation in sync. If taxonomy changes (schema v2), update exporter’s known category/action list and consider feature-flagging forward-compat behavior before widening beta cohort.

Motivation: we need customer-configurable filtering that is auditable, easy to reason about during incident response, and stable enough to map to UI + control-plane API without frequent behavior changes. Semantics (as implemented): The effective policy is evaluated in this order: validate -> normalize -> evaluate per event. If filters are absent/null, exporter passes all events (current beta default). If allowlist rules exist, an event must match at least one allow rule to be included. Deny rules always override allow rules (i.e., if an event matches any deny rule, it is excluded even if it also matches allow). Matching: category/action are exact match against canonical strings; resource matching supports resource.type exact match, and resource.id supports either exact match or a simple prefix pattern of the form "prefix:*" (to support common shapes like "workspace:*" without introducing a full glob/regex surface). If a rule specifies multiple fields, the rule matches only if all specified fields match (AND). If a filter list contains unknown categories/actions (not in the exporter’s current taxonomy allowlist), config is rejected and exporter will not start that tenant’s pipeline (surfaced as config error + status). This is intentional for beta to avoid silently dropping/including unexpected events; we can revisit “unknown = no match” for GA if we need forward-compat behavior. Notes: This PR is aligned with ADR-audit-export-filtering-model (allow/deny + deterministic evaluation) and keeps the API surface minimal. The control plane is expected to perform the same validation; exporter validation is defense-in-depth in case of stale/buggy configs. Follow-ups: (a) wire up exporter status endpoint to include “filter_validation_error” details (truncated/safe) for Console, (b) add microbench for high-volume match evaluation, (c) consider forward-compat for new categories (feature-flagged behavior) once schema v2 taxonomy is stable. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for matching + precedence - [x] Validation errors are structured and redact sensitive fields - [x] Metrics: counter for filtered_out_total by tenant + category (bounded cardinality) - [x] Docs snippet added to internal README for config examples - [ ] E2E coverage will land in ENG-5195 (separate PR)

source.title: Add Splunk HEC destination adapter (gzip compression, 429 backoff, per-token circuit breaker)
source.author: Diego Santos
source.body: Summary: Implements a new Splunk HEC destination adapter for SIEM-ready audit exports. The adapter supports gzip-compressed payloads, robust handling of downstream throttling (429) with Retry-After-aware exponential backoff + jitter, and a per-token circuit breaker to avoid hot-looping on bad credentials or persistent throttling. Motivation: Beta customers are primarily Splunk-first and we observed exporter lag spikes when HEC returns sustained 429s; this change improves delivery stability and reduces noisy retry behavior. Scope: Adapter implementation + config schema + basic unit tests + metrics hooks. Not included: Splunk indexer acknowledgment (HEC ACK) support (will track separately), advanced event field remapping beyond the audit schema’s SIEM mapping guidance. Delivery semantics: remains at-least-once; downstream Splunk consumers should dedupe on event_id if needed. Config: adds splunk_hec destination type with endpoint_url, token_ref, index/source/sourcetype (optional), max_batch_events/max_batch_bytes, flush_interval, request_timeout, max_retries, circuit_breaker thresholds and cooldown. Security: token is read via secret ref only; adapter logs redact token and never log full request bodies; destination validation ensures https and blocks loopback/private ranges when egress allowlist is enabled. Observability: adds adapter-level counters for requests, bytes, response codes, retries, breaker_open, and last_success_timestamp; integrates into existing exporter metrics namespace. Test plan: unit tests for batching, gzip encoding, retry/backoff timing (using fake clock), circuit breaker transitions; local manual validation against Splunk HEC sandbox container with artificial 429 injection. Checklist: [x] unit tests added/updated [x] lint/format [x] docs updated for destination config [x] metrics added [ ] e2e harness coverage (follow-up in ENG-5195).
commits: adapter/splunk: scaffold Splunk HEC client + destination registration
adapter/splunk: implement batching + gzip compression and Content-Encoding handling
adapter/splunk: add 429 Retry-After-aware exponential backoff with jitter
adapter/splunk: add per-token circuit breaker with cooldown and half-open probing
config: add splunk_hec destination schema + validation (https required, path normalization)
metrics: add splunk adapter counters and breaker gauges; wire into exporter registry
tests: unit tests for retry/backoff and circuit breaker; gzip payload verification
docs: internal README snippets for Splunk HEC setup and troubleshooting notes
conversation: Sean Gallagher (SRE): Can you confirm how we behave under sustained 429? Specifically: do we cap retry time and do we avoid piling up goroutines/inflight requests per tenant?

Diego Santos (author): Yes. Retries are done in the adapter worker loop (single consumer per destination partition) so we don’t spawn unbounded goroutines. Backoff is capped at 60s per attempt, max_retries defaults to 12. Additionally the per-token circuit breaker opens after 10 consecutive 429/5xx (configurable) and pauses sends for cooldown (default 5m) before probing in half-open with a single batch.

Logan Wright (Telemetry): Metrics look good, but please add labels carefully. Do we label by hec endpoint or token? Token label would be a hard no; endpoint label might be high-cardinality if customers use unique hostnames.

Diego Santos (author): Updated. Metrics label only on destination_type="splunk_hec" and tenant_id (already present across exporter metrics) plus a coarse response_class (2xx/4xx/5xx). No endpoint/token labels. Added per-destination opaque destination_id only in logs (not metrics).
Marcus Lin (Security): Please double-check that token never hits logs on errors. Also, are we including request payloads in debug logs anywhere (common gotcha with HTTP clients)?

Diego Santos (author): Found one spot where we logged headers at debug. Removed and replaced with a fixed message + request_id. Also ensured errors wrap without dumping body. Added unit test asserting token string isn’t present in adapter error formatting.

Colin O'Donnell (Solutions Eng): For Splunk customers, they often want index/sourcetype set. Can we make those optional but supported? Also can we document which HEC endpoint path we expect (/services/collector)?
Diego Santos (author): Added optional index/source/sourcetype fields. Docs now state we POST to /services/collector/event (and we’ll accept either a base URL or full path; we normalize).
Sean Gallagher (SRE): One more: does Retry-After parsing handle both seconds and HTTP date formats? Splunk sometimes returns seconds.
Diego Santos (author): Implemented seconds parsing; if parsing fails, fallback to exponential backoff. Added tests for both numeric Retry-After and missing header cases.
Logan Wright (Telemetry): Approving with note: ensure breaker_open increments once per open transition (not per failed request while open).
Diego Santos (author): Adjusted breaker_open_total to increment only on state transition; breaker_state is exposed as gauge (0=closed,1=open,2=half-open).
Marcus Lin (Security): Approved after redaction changes.
Sean Gallagher (SRE): Approved. Please coordinate rollout defaults with SLO doc in follow-up.
checks: integration-tests (splunk sandbox): skipped (not required for merge; tracked in ENG-5195)

source.title: Add dead-letter queue + guarded replay CLI for failed export batches
source.author: Diego Santos
source.description: Summary: This PR introduces a dead-letter queue (DLQ) for audit export batches that fail delivery after exhausting retries, and adds an operator-facing replay tool with guardrails. This is a reliability/safety feature for the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC / Datadog Logs / S3) to ensure we never silently drop events while still protecting the platform and customers from accidental replay storms. Context / Motivation: During downstream outages (e.g., sustained 429s from Splunk HEC or 403s from mis-scoped Datadog keys), the exporter currently retries with backoff and then surfaces a per-destination error. Without DLQ persistence we risk either (a) losing batches on process/queue churn or (b) having no safe operational path to replay specific failures once a customer fixes credentials/quotas. ENG-5164 calls for a DLQ + replay tooling with explicit operator controls. Design highlights: (1) DLQ record model: When a batch hits max_delivery_attempts OR we detect a non-retryable adapter error, we persist a DLQ record keyed by (tenant_id, export_config_id, checkpoint_range) and store the batch payload (newline-delimited JSON events) in object storage under a structured prefix. DLQ record includes: dlq_id, created_at, tenant_id, export_config_id, destination_type, reason_code, reason_detail (redacted), first_event_ts, last_event_ts, event_count, byte_size, last_attempt_at, attempt_count, and a payload_ref. (2) At-least-once compatibility: Replays may cause duplicate deliveries. We intentionally do not attempt exactly-once; downstream should dedup using event_id (per ADR on delivery semantics). Replay CLI logs a reminder and requires an explicit --ack-duplicates flag. (3) Operator replay CLI: Adds bin/audit-exporter-replay (Go) to list DLQ items and replay in a controlled fashion. Guardrails include: required --tenant-id and --export-config-id; default is dry-run; interactive confirmation unless --yes is provided; bounded selection via --since/--until and --max-items; token-bucket rate limiting (--max-events-per-minute) to avoid spiking adapters; refuses to replay if export config is disabled OR if exporter feature flag is off for that tenant (unless --override-feature-flag is provided, which is restricted to on-call breakglass role). (4) Safety checks: Replay validates destination configuration hash matches the DLQ record by default (prevents replaying old payloads into a newly re-pointed sink). Can be overridden with --ignore-config-hash-mismatch for incident response, but emits a prominent warning. (5) Redaction: DLQ reason_detail is run through the same redaction helper used by adapter logs to avoid accidentally persisting tokens/keys/PII. Payloads contain audit events only (already subject to audit schema/PII policy); we do not include request/response bodies. (6) Observability: Adds metrics: audit_exporter_dlq_enqueued_total{tenant_id_hash,destination_type,reason_code}, audit_exporter_dlq_backlog_items{destination_type}, audit_exporter_dlq_backlog_events{destination_type}, audit_exporter_dlq_replay_total{result}, and audit_exporter_dlq_replay_latency_seconds. Also adds structured logs for DLQ enqueue and replay runs (with correlation_id). User-visible / Ops workflow: - When an adapter can’t deliver a batch and we exceed retry limits, exporter transitions the batch to DLQ and advances the live pipeline checkpoint to prevent system-wide backpressure. - On-call or Support can instruct customer to fix auth/limits, then run replay for the impacted tenant/config/time window. - Replay is incremental and rate-limited; any failures during replay are re-DLQ’d with a new reason_code (replay_failed_*). Example commands: - List backlog: ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq list --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --until 2025-02-09T06:00:00Z - Dry-run replay (default): ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq replay --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --max-items 10 - Execute replay with explicit acknowledgements: ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq replay --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --max-items 50 --max-events-per-minute 12000 --ack-duplicates --yes Testing: - Unit tests for DLQ persistence, reason_code mapping, and redaction - Integration tests using adapter stubs: force 429/403/non-retryable to verify DLQ enqueue and payload roundtrip - Manual: replayed a synthetic backlog into Splunk stub with rate limiting and verified dedup by event_id Related: - Linear: ENG-5164 - ADR: delivery semantics and idempotency (at-least-once + event_id) - Support context: SUP-18531 (perceived missing events during throttling) Notes / Follow-ups: - Dashboard panels/alerts for DLQ backlog are tracked separately (we add metrics here, panels in observability-pack). - For beta, replay CLI is intended for on-call / SRE / Support escalation only; we are not exposing self-serve replay in Console.

dlq: persist failed batch payload to object storage with structured key prefix
exporter: route terminal adapter failures to DLQ and advance checkpoint safely
cli: add audit-exporter-replay dlq list/replay commands with dry-run default
cli: add guardrails (tenant/config required, confirmations, config hash check, rate limiting)
obs: add DLQ enqueue/backlog/replay metrics + structured logs
tests: add DLQ unit tests + adapter failure integration tests
changed_areas: exporter pipeline: failure handling + checkpoint advancement

source.review_conversation: Sean Gallagher (SRE): Can we confirm advancing the live checkpoint after DLQ enqueue won’t violate at-least-once? If we advance, aren’t we skipping events? Diego Santos (author): We’re not skipping generation; we’re persisting the batch payload + checkpoint range and treating replay as the at-least-once path. Without advancing, a stuck tenant can backpressure worker resources and starve other tenants. This matches ADR guidance: preserve payload, allow replay, accept duplicates. Marcus Lin (Security): Please ensure DLQ reason_detail doesn’t store adapter error blobs that may include tokens. Also confirm payload only contains audit events (no request bodies). Diego: Added redaction on reason_detail and a unit test that simulates Splunk error strings containing 'Authorization: Splunk <token>' and verifies it is redacted. Payload is the normalized audit event schema only; no HTTP request/response is serialized. Hanae Suzuki (Secrets/KMS): Where are DLQ payloads encrypted? Diego: Uses existing object storage client with SSE-KMS enabled in beta environments; added config validation to refuse startup if DLQ payload store is configured without encryption in hosted/dedicated. Aisha Bello (SRE): CLI needs stronger guardrails. Can we require --ack-duplicates and make dry-run default? Also add a cap to prevent replaying >N events unless explicitly set. Diego: Updated CLI: dry-run is default; execution requires both --ack-duplicates and --yes; added --max-items default 100 and hard cap 10k unless --breakglass is used. Logan Wright (Telemetry): Metrics label cardinality: do not label by raw tenant_id. Diego: Updated to tenant_id_hash and destination_type/reason_code only. Added note in docs about support workflow for identifying tenant via internal tooling. Approvals: Sean approved after guardrails changes; Marcus approved after redaction changes; Hanae approved after encryption validation; Logan approved after label change.

source.title: Support backup-oriented batch export mode (pagination + retention guardrails)
source.author: Kevin Osei
source.description:
Context: Private DR / compliance asks require a repeatable, non-interactive way to export audit logs for backup/retention evidence. The existing exporter is optimized for ad-hoc, interactive exports and can be slow/fragile when used in scheduled jobs (offset-based pagination, no checkpointing, limited safeguards around retention boundaries).

This PR adds a backup-oriented batch export mode designed for scheduled backups:
- New CLI subcommand: `audit-log-exporter export batch`
- Cursor-based pagination using the API’s stable `after_id`/`next_cursor` semantics (no offset)
- Checkpointing/resume: writes a checkpoint token to disk periodically; supports `--resume` to continue without duplicating output
- Retention guardrails: validates requested time window against server-reported retention (if available) and fails fast with actionable messaging; explicit override flag required to proceed when outside retention
- Bounded exports: `--max-pages`, `--page-size`, and `--max-records` to prevent runaway jobs
- Deterministic output layout suitable for backup:
  - JSONL lines (one event per line)
  - Rotates files by `--chunk-records` or `--chunk-bytes`
  - Writes a manifest containing record counts, min/max event timestamps, and sha256 checksums
- Backoff + retry tuning for long-running jobs, with explicit handling for rate limits and transient gateway errors

Notes:

source.conversation:
Marcus Lin (Security): Can we confirm this doesn’t ever write secret material to disk (tokens/headers)? Also, how do we prevent exporting outside retention from silently producing partial backups?

Kevin Osei (Author): Confirmed: we never persist headers; checkpoint only contains the server cursor token + last exported event id and timestamp window. Added explicit retention guardrails: if the server reports retention days and the requested `--start` is older than allowed, we hard-fail with a message explaining the likely cause. There’s an override flag `--allow-outside-retention` for break-glass (still emits a warning + records the fact in the manifest).

Sean Gallagher (SRE): Batch jobs need to be resumable. If the job is killed mid-run, do we get duplicates? Also please add hard bounds; we’ve seen runaway loops in other tooling.

Kevin Osei (Author): Added checkpointing (default every 5k records / configurable). On resume, we re-open the last output file (if present) and continue from the last committed cursor; we also track `last_event_id` to protect against repeated pages when the API returns an overlapping cursor under retry. Added `--max-pages`, `--max-records`, and `--page-size` bounds.

Hanae Suzuki (Security/KMS): The docs should be explicit that the exporter doesn’t do client-side encryption. Customers will ask.

Kevin Osei (Author): Updated `docs/backup_batch_export.md` and README with a dedicated note and examples for S3 SSE-KMS / GCS CMEK.

Connor O'Brien (Release/Packaging): For air-gapped bundles, we want deterministic file names and a manifest suitable for checksum verification.

Kevin Osei (Author): Output now uses deterministic naming: `audit-logs_<start>_<end>_partNNNN.jsonl.gz` (or uncompressed if `--no-compress`), plus `manifest.json` (counts + sha256). The manifest is stable across reruns if the same event set is exported.

Marcus Lin: One more ask: can the manifest include the exporter version + command line args? Helps evidence.

Kevin Osei (Author): Added `exporter_version`, `invoked_at`, and `args` to the manifest (args redacted for token flags).

Sean Gallagher: Looks good. Please ensure retries don’t reorder output.

Kevin Osei (Author): Output ordering is preserved by only appending pages in order; retries re-fetch the same page and we de-dup by event id at page boundary. Added tests to cover the overlap case.

Approvals:
- Sean Gallagher: Approved
- Marcus Lin: Approved
- Hanae Suzuki: Approved
- Connor O'Brien: Approved

- The exporter continues to support the existing `export` path unchanged.

source.title: Implement S3 destination adapter (batching, partitioning, checkpoints, retries)
source.author: Diego Santos
source.description:
Context: This PR adds the first destination adapter for the SIEM-ready audit export beta: S3 delivery. The adapter is built around (a) deterministic partitioning for cost/operability, (b) bounded batching for throughput, and (c) at-least-once delivery via checkpoint persistence.

Linear: ENG-5144

What’s included:
- New S3 destination adapter implementing the exporter Destination interface
- Batching policy: flush on max_events, max_bytes, or flush_interval (defaults tuned for beta)
- Partitioning scheme (configurable prefix + time partitioning + tenant scoping) to avoid hot prefixes and keep search-friendly layouts
- Checkpoint persistence for at-least-once semantics (write object -> persist checkpoint). Cursor is (emitted_at, event_id) and is treated as an opaque ordering token by downstream
- Retry/backoff for S3 throttling/transient errors (SlowDown/503/timeouts) with jitter and a max retry cap; non-retryable errors go to DLQ (DLQ plumbing is stubbed behind an interface; full operator tooling comes in ENG-5164)
- Minimal metrics around adapter throughput, batch size, upload latency, and retry counts
- Unit tests for batching edge cases (size boundaries, timer flush, retry classification) and a small local dev harness doc

Out of scope (follow-ups):
- SSE-KMS + cross-account assume role (tracked in ENG-5148; implemented in PR #1068)
- Integration tests with real AWS / localstack matrix (tracked in ENG-5195)
- Console UI + config CRUD (control-plane work)

Implementation details:
- Object format: newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON) where each line is a single canonical audit event payload (already emitted by upstream as JSON). We intentionally avoid wrapping in a top-level array to support streaming/partial reads in customer tooling.
- Object key layout (default): s3://<bucket>/<prefix>/tenant_id=<tenant>/workspace_id=<workspace>/dt=YYYY-MM-DD/hour=HH/<destination_id>_<unix_ms>_<batch_seq>.ndjson
  - tenant_id/workspace_id are included to make customer-side permissions/audits easier when exporting multiple workspaces into a shared bucket.
  - dt/hour partitions keep file counts reasonable for typical SIEM collectors.
- Checkpoint store: uses the exporter’s checkpoint repository abstraction backed by the existing service DB (per-tenant scoped). Each destination has a single monotonically advancing cursor.
  - We only advance the checkpoint after the upload succeeds.
  - If checkpoint update fails after upload, we will re-upload on restart (duplicates are expected; downstream should dedup on event_id).
- Retry policy: exponential backoff with decorrelated jitter. We treat 429/SlowDown/5xx/timeouts as retryable. 403/AccessDenied and 400/BadRequest are non-retryable and are surfaced as destination health errors.
- Guardrails: adapter enforces a max object size (hard cap) and will split batches accordingly; also validates bucket + prefix format early so we fail fast during config validation.

Operational notes:
- Adapter emits structured logs with destination_id/tenant_id/workspace_id, but never logs event payloads.
- Added a per-destination circuit-breaker-ish counter (consecutive failures) used by the exporter loop to mark destination unhealthy (full auto-pausing is in PR #1119).

Testing:
- Unit tests for batching and retry classification (go test ./...)
- Manual smoke test using AWS dev bucket documented in docs/dev/s3-smoke-test.md

Rollout:
- No runtime behavior change unless S3 destination type is configured. Feature flag gating is handled upstream in control-plane; this PR is safe to deploy to exporter environments ahead of time.

notable_snippets:
Default S3 key format:
key := fmt.Sprintf("%s/tenant_id=%s/workspace_id=%s/dt=%s/hour=%s/%s_%d_%06d.ndjson", prefix, tenantID, workspaceID, day, hour, destinationID, nowUnixMs, batchSeq)
Batch flush conditions:
if batch.events >= cfg.MaxEvents || batch.bytes >= cfg.MaxBytes || time.Since(batch.startedAt) >= cfg.FlushInterval { flush() }
Checkpoint update ordering:
err := s3.PutObject(...)
if err == nil { checkpointRepo.Advance(ctx, tenantID, destinationID, cursor) }
review_conversation: Sean Gallagher (SRE): Please make sure we don’t create tiny objects under low volume tenants; can we enforce a minimum flush interval and log batch stats? Author: Added flush_interval default of 5s and metrics/log line on flush (events/bytes/upload_ms).

Logan Wright (Observability): Metric names should follow exporter namespace conventions; also watch label cardinality (workspace_id is risky). Author: Renamed metrics to audit_exporter_destination_* and removed workspace_id from metric labels (kept in logs only).

Michael Nguyen (Persistence): Cursor shape needs to be stable and comparable; please don’t rely on DB ordering of UUIDs. Author: Cursor explicitly uses (emitted_at, event_id) and we only compare emitted_at first; event_id is a tie-breaker string for deterministic pagination upstream.

Hanae Suzuki (Security): Ensure we don’t log bucket names with customer identifiers in error paths; also note encryption defaults. Author: Redacted bucket/prefix in error logs (hashed) and clarified in description that SSE-KMS/cross-account is follow-up; current behavior relies on bucket policy defaults (no explicit SSE headers yet).
Reviewer request: Add validation for prefix traversal (../) and empty prefix handling. Author: Added prefix sanitization + unit tests.
release_notes: Adds S3 destination adapter for audit log export with batching, partitioned object keys, checkpointing, and retry/backoff.
No behavior change unless an S3 destination is configured.
